stories.headline: Measles case confirmed at Denver International Airport and Littleton church
stories.summary:
A traveler with a confirmed measles case passed through Denver International Airport and attended a Sunday service at St. Mary's Catholic Church in Littleton while infectious, Colorado public health officials announced Tuesday. The passenger arrived Feb. 21 on Frontier flight 1279 from Miami, landing at gate A83 at 9:15 a.m., and attended the church service and reception at 6853 S. Prince St. between 9:30 a.m. and 2 p.m. Anyone present in those locations during those windows may have been exposed.

The traveler returned to DIA Sunday night and departed on Frontier flight 1280 to Miami at 11:29 p.m. from gate A76, meaning anyone at the airport between 9:30 p.m. Sunday and 1:30 a.m. Monday faces potential exposure. Public health officials noted that because the passenger is fully vaccinated, the risk of transmission is lower but not eliminated.

The Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment opened free measles vaccination clinics Wednesday at the Arapahoe County Public Health Department in Englewood and at St. Mary's Catholic Church. Receiving a vaccine within 72 hours of exposure may prevent measles or reduce its severity.

Colorado recorded 36 measles cases in 2025, the highest single-year total since at least 1996, with most involving unvaccinated individuals. This marks the first confirmed case in the state in 2026, logged in Arapahoe County.

stories.headline: Grass fire injures five, forces evacuations and closes I-25 in Thornton
stories.summary:
A vegetation fire ignited near 84th Avenue and Huron Street in Thornton at approximately 11:30 a.m. on Feb. 25 under Red Flag conditions, spreading rapidly eastward through a greenbelt between a residential neighborhood and commercial zone. The blaze burned approximately 10 acres before being fully contained at 2:07 p.m., with fire crews from Thornton, Westminster, Adams County, North Glenn, Federal Heights, South Adams and other jurisdictions responding.

Five people were injured in connection with the fire, including four firefighters and one civilian. Thornton Fire Chief Stephen Kelley described the injuries as non-critical and confirmed that no residential structures were lost, though some businesses on the southern edge of the fire sustained damage that was not believed to be structural.

Law enforcement conducted door-to-door evacuations of nearby residents, and a local high school was fully evacuated as a precaution. Sections of I-25 were closed due to billowing smoke that severely reduced visibility. Crews remained on scene overnight to monitor for any reignition of hotspots.

"This was a dynamic, rapidly spreading event that challenged many of the departments in the north area," Chief Kelley said at a press conference. The cause of the fire remains under investigation.

stories.headline: Man arrested in Kansas on murder charges in Denver Valentine's Day shooting
stories.summary:
A 20-year-old man suspected of killing a man and wounding a woman in a Denver shooting on Valentine's Day was arrested Friday in Kansas and is being held at the Wyandotte County Detention Center on a Denver homicide warrant. Yeanbraiker Yriarte-Valera faces charges of first-degree murder, four counts of attempted first-degree murder, four counts of first-degree assault and a violent crime sentence enhancer, according to Denver court records.

Denver police responded to the shooting in the 1500 block of West Maple Avenue at approximately 5:15 a.m. on Feb. 14, finding a man dead at the scene and a woman shot in the ankle who was transported to a hospital. Investigators believe the shooting stemmed from a fight that broke out at a party in the area and escalated when at least two people opened fire.

Yriarte-Valera is believed to have fired the shot that killed the male victim. A second suspect, who allegedly shot the female victim, had not been publicly identified as of Tuesday. Yriarte-Valera's first Colorado court date had not yet been scheduled.

Anyone with information on the shooting is asked to contact Metro Denver Crime Stoppers at 720-913-7867.

stories.headline: Two defendants plead guilty to second-degree murder in 2024 Denver Aquarium shooting
stories.summary:
Two men accused of fatally shooting 19-year-old Dacien Salazar outside the Denver Aquarium on Valentine's Day 2024 have pleaded guilty to second-degree murder and are set to be sentenced March 6. Jason Trujillo, 19, pleaded guilty Jan. 16, 2026, and Antonio Vasquez, 21, entered his plea Dec. 19, 2025, according to the Denver District Attorney's Office.

Salazar and two friends had traveled from Pueblo to the aquarium on Feb. 14, 2024, when a black sedan pulled up and a masked person inside opened fire. Salazar was taken to a hospital where he later died. Investigators connected the shooting to prior Facebook threats made against Salazar, including one involving Trujillo.

Trujillo and Vasquez were arrested in May 2024 on first-degree murder charges after surveillance footage placed them at a nearby hotel before and after the shooting, and cell phone records tied Vasquez to the scene. An anonymous tip helped investigators identify Trujillo's involvement.

The case drew significant public attention in Denver after the brazen daytime shooting outside a popular family attraction. Both defendants were originally charged with first-degree murder before reaching plea agreements.

stories.headline: Denver and Colorado business leaders welcome Supreme Court tariff ruling, warn more trade actions likely
stories.summary:
Denver and Colorado business leaders responded swiftly after the U.S. Supreme Court struck down the Trump administration's tariffs in a 6-3 decision, saying the ruling restores critical predictability for companies across the state. The decision centered on tariffs imposed under an emergency powers law, including sweeping reciprocal tariffs levied on nearly every country.

The Denver Metro Chamber of Commerce and the Colorado Chamber of Commerce both issued statements praising the ruling for providing regulatory clarity. Colorado Chamber President Loren Furman noted that many member businesses across sectors had experienced supply chain disruptions, increased overhead costs and uncertainty as a result of the tariffs.

Despite the ruling, several business leaders cautioned that relief may be temporary. Matthew Groves, president and CEO of one trade group, said the administration retains multiple avenues to apply tariffs and called the ruling "temporary relief." President Trump announced he would sign an executive order imposing a 10% global tariff rate above existing tariffs hours after the decision, calling the court's ruling "incorrect."

Colorado industries affected by the prior tariffs included wheat producers, bicycle retailers and automotive dealerships. The head of the Colorado Automobile Dealers Association said the group supports any action that makes vehicles more affordable for consumers.

stories.headline: Laura Clellan confirmed as first female director of Colorado Parks and Wildlife since 2011 merger
stories.summary:
Colorado Parks and Wildlife commissioners voted unanimously Monday to confirm Laura Clellan as the agency's permanent director, making her the first woman to hold the position since the parks and wildlife divisions merged in 2011. Clellan had been serving as acting director since Dec. 1 after being selected from approximately 150 applicants.

Clellan brings more than three decades of military service to the role, including multiple overseas deployments and service as adjutant general of the Colorado Department of Military and Veterans Affairs. She achieved the rank of major general in the Colorado Army National Guard, overseeing 5,600 citizen soldiers, and previously worked with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service in Lakewood.

She assumes leadership of the agency during a particularly contentious period. The federal government has halted Colorado's wolf reintroduction program, stakeholder groups are in conflict over furbearer management rules, and some hunting advocacy organizations have expressed concern that the Polis administration is sidelining their interests through appointments and policy positions.

Despite the political tensions, conservation groups and hunting advocates both expressed measured optimism about Clellan's appointment. Dan Gates, executive director of Coloradans for Responsible Wildlife Management, said his group is confident she will provide equitable transparency and accountability in leading the agency.

stories.headline: Historic Loretto Heights administration building to become 50 rent-to-own apartments in $70M project
stories.summary:
Developer Commún plans to convert the historic Administration Building at Denver's Loretto Heights campus into 50 rent-to-own affordable apartments as part of a broader $70 million campus redevelopment effort. The project represents a rare application of the rent-to-own model in Denver's affordable housing landscape, giving residents a path toward homeownership.

The first two floors of the building will be activated for community programming, including a business incubator, while the remainder of the structure will be converted to residential use. A historic chapel that is part of the building will be preserved and continue operating as a wedding and event venue.

Loretto Heights, a former Catholic college campus in southwest Denver, has been the subject of redevelopment planning for several years. The Administration Building is among the campus's most architecturally significant structures, and its adaptive reuse has been a priority for community stakeholders and preservationists.

The project reflects a broader trend of developers pursuing mixed-use adaptive reuse of historic Denver buildings to address the city's affordable housing shortage while preserving significant structures.

stories.headline: Denver Pavilions' future to be studied by national Urban Land Institute panel this spring
stories.summary:
The Denver Downtown Development Authority has engaged the Urban Land Institute to send an advisory panel to Denver from April 13-17 to produce recommendations on the future of the Denver Pavilions mall and adjacent parking lots. The DDDA is paying ULI $100,000 for the study, which will draw five or six panelists from outside the region.

The DDDA purchased the 350,000-square-foot mall at 500 16th St. in December for $37 million from Gart Properties, which had defaulted on the property's loan, and paid an additional $23 million for two adjacent parking lots from Brookfield Properties. The mall is approximately 60% leased and generates $5.3 million in annual rent, while the parking generates another $3.2 million annually.

Ahead of the ULI visit, the DDDA has issued two requests for proposals due March 13 — one seeking retail, dining and entertainment leasing consultants and a second seeking architecture and urban planning firms to reimagine the Pavilions as "the catalytic centerpiece of a revitalized Upper Downtown." Chief Projects Officer Bill Mosher said his preference is to avoid signing major leases and transfer the asset to the private sector as quickly as possible.

The Pavilions has long been considered an underperforming anchor on the 16th Street Mall. Two escalators remain out of service and the mall currently lacks a dedicated leasing team, with prior owner Gart Properties continuing to manage the property for $379,000.

stories.headline: $132 million affordable housing development breaks ground in Denver's Globeville neighborhood
stories.summary:
Evergreen Real Estate Group and Globeville Redevelopment Partners have broken ground on a $132 million mixed-use affordable housing development in Denver's Globeville neighborhood. The project will transform a city-owned parcel on the site of a former car dealership into housing for families earning between 30% and 80% of the area median income.

Globeville is one of Denver's most historically underinvested neighborhoods, situated near the confluence of I-25 and I-70 and long affected by industrial land use and highway infrastructure. The development represents one of the larger affordable housing investments in the area in recent memory.

The project adds to a pipeline of affordable and mixed-income housing developments advancing across the Denver metro as city and state officials continue to grapple with a persistent shortage of attainable housing. Details on the number of units and anticipated completion timeline were not fully disclosed in available project documents.

The development follows a broader city strategy of repurposing publicly owned land parcels for affordable housing, an approach Denver has increasingly deployed to accelerate production without the cost of land acquisition.

stories.headline: Heat pump installations in Colorado more than doubled in 2025, reaching market tipping point
stories.summary:
The number of heat pumps installed across five major Colorado utility service areas more than doubled in 2025 to 14,225 units, representing a twelvefold increase over three years, according to new data from the Energy Efficiency Business Coalition. Industry executives and installers say the technology has reached mainstream market acceptance after years as a niche product.

Xcel Energy, Colorado's largest gas and electricity supplier, issued 10,640 heat pump rebates worth $57 million in 2025, with Denver, Boulder and Littleton among the most active markets. The Platte River Power Authority, serving Fort Collins, Longmont and surrounding communities, saw its heat pump rebates nearly quadruple to 1,256, totaling $2.2 million.

Contractors and utility executives credit the surge to a combination of factors: installers have become more comfortable with the technology, sales staff have learned to stack available rebates and incentives to make the upfront cost more competitive, and cold-weather heat pump designs have improved substantially. Heat pumps range from $15,000 to $30,000 depending on system size.

Nationally, heat pumps outsold gas and oil furnaces in 2025 for the second consecutive year, with 3.642 million units sold compared to 3.28 million furnaces, according to the Air-Conditioning, Heating and Refrigeration Institute. The Energy Efficiency Business Coalition is targeting heat pumps capturing 50% to 80% of the total Colorado market by 2030.

stories.headline: Broncos bring back John Morton as offensive passing-game coordinator ahead of 2026 season
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos have hired John Morton as offensive passing-game coordinator, reuniting him with head coach Sean Payton for a third stint together, a source confirmed to The Denver Post. Morton had served in the same role with Denver in 2023 and 2024 before leaving for an offensive coordinator position with the Detroit Lions in 2025.

Morton was stripped of play-calling duties by Detroit midseason and fired after the Lions finished 9-8. Payton subsequently brought him back to Denver as a consultant during the Broncos' playoff run, and Morton was credited with helping install a goal-line play for offensive tackle Frank Crum that produced a touchdown in the team's 33-30 overtime divisional win over the Buffalo Bills.

The hire completes a significant reshaping of Payton's offensive staff following the AFC Championship Game loss. Payton fired longtime offensive coordinator Joe Lombardi two days after that defeat, elevated quarterbacks coach Davis Webb to offensive coordinator, promoted offensive quality-control coach Logan Kilgore to the quarterback coaching vacancy and hired Ronald Curry as wide-receivers coach.

Morton and Payton have now worked together across five seasons spanning two franchises, dating back to Morton's time as New Orleans Saints receivers coach in Payton's first year there in 2006. Whether Morton will have a formal play-calling role alongside Webb in 2026 has not been determined.

stories.headline: MSU Denver launches fellowship to support and retain Black male educators in Denver and Aurora schools
stories.summary:
Metropolitan State University of Denver has launched the Black Male Educator Fellowship, nicknamed "The Den," to provide professional development, networking and community support for Black male teachers working in Denver Public Schools and Aurora Public Schools. The program is a collaboration among MSU Denver's Call Me MiSTER initiative, the university's Office of Education Solutions, both school districts and the Colorado Men of Color Collaborative.

Program director Dr. Rashad Anderson said isolation is a significant driver of attrition among Black male teachers, noting that Black residents make up just 6% of Colorado's population and many Black educators are the only teacher of color in their school building. Anderson said the fellowship is designed to create a sense of brotherhood and professional community that helps retain educators who might otherwise leave the classroom.

National data underscore the need: 40% of Colorado school districts employ no teacher of color, and 62% have no Black male teacher at all, according to the National Association of State Boards of Education. Research consistently shows that students of all backgrounds benefit from exposure to teachers from diverse backgrounds.

Call Me MiSTER participant Kaheem Heath, an aspiring teacher, said he has never had a Black male teacher in his own schooling and sees the fellowship as critical to changing that reality for the next generation. "I just want to follow his footsteps as we inspire other Black men to join the program as well, to get into those classrooms," Heath said.

comic.teaser: "“Well, according to the dictionary, I’m just a large, flightless bird from Ea..." + 3 more
